title: America’s “direct return” doctrine—and what it means for the world – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Jim O'Brien, a Dayton Peace Agreement drafter, argues the Trump administration's new Western Balkans policy of 'direct return for American companies' is replacing 30 years of institution-building with self-dealing that weakens Bosnia's Dayton institutions and risks reigniting conflict. He uses the Bosnia gas-pipeline ('Southern Interconnection') and Serbia's Russian-owned Nis oil refinery as case studies, and prescribes that Europe acquire a veto over US commercial deals—via an empowered high representative in BiH and EU sanctions authority over Nis.

insights.claim: The US has announced that its Western Balkans decisions will henceforth be guided by 'direct return' for American companies, replacing the post-1995 Dayton goal of open-ended institution building.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Signals a transactional US foreign policy where commercial deals override stability—reprices political risk for any asset whose value depends on US institutional commitments.
insights.quote: henceforth its decisions are to be guided by the need to pursue “direct return” for American companies
insights.locator: Last month the US announced a new policy

insights.claim: Politically connected Americans are earning money by weakening Dayton's international institutions with active US government support, with each act of institutional erosion creating further commercial opportunities.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: A self-reinforcing erosion loop means institutional decay in the Balkans is likely to accelerate, raising regional conflict and energy-supply risk.
insights.quote: Each act of chipping away at those institutions creates yet more commercial opportunities.
insights.locator: But this announcement merely reflected

insights.claim: O'Brien argues the 'direct return' announcement is less a new policy than a formalisation of self-dealing already underway under the second Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Treating the doctrine as descriptive, not aspirational, implies the behaviour is entrenched and will recur across other theatres.
insights.quote: The Trump administration’s policy of “direct return for US companies” is more a statement of existing practice.
insights.locator: Bosnia and Herzegovina: Pipeline of deals

insights.claim: The US lifted sanctions on a pro-MAGA Bosnian politician despite his continued sanctionable activity, including a $4m lobbying contract aimed at securing 'the independence of Republika Srpska from Bosnia and Herzegovina'.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Sanctions relief decoupled from behaviour shows US designations becoming politically transactional—reduces the deterrent value of US sanctions as a policy tool.
insights.quote: a single contract of $4m aiming at securing “the independence of Republika Srpska from Bosnia and Herzegovina.”
insights.locator: To illustrate: last year

insights.claim: An American company founded by the brother of a sanctions-lobbyist was named owner of rights to build the 'Southern Interconnection' gas pipeline despite being the sole bidder with no pipeline experience.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: A non-competitive award to an inexperienced insider firm is a marker of how 'direct return' deals are structured—watch for similar single-bidder infrastructure awards.
insights.quote: the company was the only bidder, and it has no experience in building or operating a pipeline
insights.locator: Soon after, the brother of a lobbyist

insights.claim: The US demanded and obtained the resignation of Bosnia's high representative, who held authority over a property dispute critical to the pipeline and had not favoured a resolution sympathetic to the pipeline owners.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Removing the key institutional check to unlock a deal shows US willingness to dismantle peace-treaty offices for commercial ends—direct threat to Dayton stability.
insights.quote: the US demanded the resignation of the high representative, which duly happened
insights.locator: Besides the probable enabling of corrupt practices

insights.claim: A new, more amenable high representative could enable transactions on more than 1,000 disputed properties—'a cornucopia in Bosnian terms'—benefiting politicians who undermined the peace deal.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: The scale of disputed-property value at stake explains the intensity of US pressure and the corruption incentive—a structural driver of continued institutional capture.
insights.quote: transactions on more than 1,000 disputed properties, a cornucopia in Bosnian terms
insights.locator: A new, presumably more amenable

insights.claim: Rumours in BiH suggest the US company holding the pipeline concession is likely to sell its interest quickly—'money for virtually nothing'—with an eye on possible Democratic congressional probes after 2027.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: A flip-and-exit pattern means the asset's strategic stewardship is secondary to extraction—raises odds the concession lands with adverse owners.
insights.quote: rumours in BiH suggest the US company with the gas pipeline concession is likely to sell its interest quickly
insights.locator: But here is a further twist

insights.claim: If the US pipeline company sells, local elites opposed to the pipeline or Russian entities may acquire the rights, steering the region deeper into the very Russian-energy dependence the pipeline was meant to address.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Self-defeating outcome where a US 'security' deal increases Russian energy leverage—watch the buyer of any flipped Balkan energy concession as a Russia-exposure signal.
insights.quote: The same administration’s pursuit of “direct return for American companies” may thus steer the region deeper into that vulnerability.
insights.locator: This swapping of ownership could create

insights.claim: Last month the administration itself labelled corruption and dependence on Russian energy interests a 'strategic vulnerability' the pipeline aims to address.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: The administration's own framing creates an accountability hook if its deals worsen Russian dependence—a contradiction Europe can exploit diplomatically.
insights.quote: both corruption and dependence on Russian energy interests have been a “strategic vulnerability” in the region
insights.locator: Either way, just last month

insights.claim: Serbia's Nis oil refinery—the most lucrative company in Serbia and crucial to regional oil supplies—is Russian-owned and has been under US sanctions since October 2024.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: A Russian-owned, sanctioned, regionally critical refinery is a live energy-supply chokepoint—its ownership resolution is a tradable catalyst for Balkan fuel markets.
insights.quote: the Nis oil refinery—the most lucrative company in Serbia, and which is owned by Russian entities—has been under American sanctions since October 2024
insights.locator: Serbia: Signal for the future

insights.claim: The Trump administration has zigzagged on Nis—relaxing then re-enforcing sanctions and jeopardising regional oil supplies—before agreeing in December 2025 to let Hungarian state oil company MOL negotiate for the refinery.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Erratic sanctions enforcement injects supply uncertainty into regional fuel pricing; MOL's involvement is the key variable to track for resolution.
insights.quote: It relaxed and then enforced sanctions on Nis, jeopardising oil supplies throughout the region
insights.locator: Instead, the Trump administration has zigged and zagged

insights.claim: MOL intends to operate the Nis refinery itself, but the sale has been approved by neither Russia nor the US administration.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: The deal is stalled pending dual US-Russia approval—an unresolved overhang on regional supply and on MOL's Balkan expansion.
insights.quote: MOL still intends to operate the refinery itself, but the sale has not been approved by either Russia or the US administration.
insights.locator: This administration renewed its approval

insights.claim: How the administration resolves Nis is a tell for its remaining two years: backing MOL/a pro-Western consortium, letting Russians keep it, or requiring a US purchaser as proof of 'direct return' in action.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Nis becomes a real-time barometer of US-Russia dealmaking appetite—an early read on broader policy toward Ukraine and energy assets.
insights.quote: What the administration does about Nis may tell us something about its remaining two years in power.
insights.locator: What the administration does about Nis

insights.claim: Allowing Russians to keep Nis would suggest the administration still believes US-Russia commercial deals remain possible, which O'Brien calls 'ominous for Ukraine'.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Links a Balkan refinery outcome to US-Russia rapprochement risk over Ukraine—a cross-asset signal for Ukraine-exposed and European defence trades.
insights.quote: This last option would suggest the administration continues to believe commercial deals between the US and Russia remain possible (which could be ominous for Ukraine).
insights.locator: It could support MOL

insights.claim: O'Brien's core prescription is that Europe must secure a veto over the commercial deals motivating US policy, then negotiate a reasonable resolution on Bosnia's governance.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Frames European leverage as deal-veto power rather than rhetoric—a concrete policy lever that, if used, would reshape Balkan asset ownership outcomes.
insights.quote: The core challenge for Europe is to get a veto on the commercial deals that motivate US policy.
insights.locator: Acquiring European vetoes

insights.claim: Europe's best BiH option is an empowered high representative mandated to block corrupt or opaque deals on state property and critical infrastructure, with the mandate mattering more than the person.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: The high-representative mandate is the institutional choke point—its terms determine whether the pipeline and property deals proceed.
insights.quote: The issue is less who the high representative is...than what the mandate is on property issues.
insights.locator: In BiH, Europe’s best option

insights.claim: Securing US agreement on an empowered high representative will likely require France to lead given Macron's personal involvement, and a 'distasteful deal' giving the US some of what it wants.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: France as lead negotiator is the likely vector for any BiH settlement—watch Paris, not Brussels, for movement on the Balkan file.
insights.quote: This may mean seeing France take the leading role, given President Emmanuel Macron’s personal involvement
insights.locator: Winning US agreement on an empowered

insights.claim: For Serbia, O'Brien argues the European Commission can on its own authority use existing sanctions powers to require transfer of Nis to an acceptable buyer, having designated the refinery's largest shareholder in 2022 but held back from ordering a transfer.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: The EU already holds the legal tool to force Nis's sale—its inaction is a choice, so any future use of that authority is a step-change catalyst for the refinery.
insights.quote: use its sanctions authorities to require the transfer of Nis to a buyer it deems acceptable
insights.locator: In Serbia, the EU has a simple way forward

insights.claim: The EU could reinforce its Nis offer by backing a European-led buyer consortium and tying pro-European ownership requirements to Serbia's single-market and energy/transport integration tools.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Conditioning EU integration on asset ownership is a lever that could redirect a strategic refinery to European hands—relevant for European energy-infra investors.
insights.quote: supporting a European-led consortium to buy the refinery
insights.locator: The EU could reinforce its offer

insights.claim: Small sums from these deals can still unravel peace because power and money flow to actors thriving on the margins of the rule of law in the grey areas left by the 1990s wars.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Reframes a minor-looking commercial deal as a peace-stability risk—the downside is conflict re-ignition, not just corruption.
insights.quote: The money flowing from these deals seems too small to jeopardise peace. But power and money will flow towards people who thrive on the margins of the rule of law.
insights.locator: The money flowing from these deals

insights.claim: O'Brien presents the Balkans as a template: the administration's 'money-for-a-few' choices could indicate how it will act in its final years in Gaza, Ukraine, Iran, the South Caucasus and worldwide.
insights.speaker: Jim O’Brien
insights.so_what: Generalises the doctrine globally—investors in any US-mediated geopolitical situation should price in transactional self-dealing as a base case.
insights.quote: This is how peace will unravel in the age of Trump.
insights.locator: Slight return
